name: Approximation Algorithms

x.io: Instance of an NP-hard optimisation problem → a feasible solution with a proven ratio to the optimum.
preconditions: Exact optimum is intractable at the required scale
A measurable objective with a known/boundable optimum
correctness: Approximation ratio ρ proven for all instances
Feasibility always maintained, only optimality relaxed
x.stability: Deterministic ratio (or expected ratio for randomised approximations).
x.parallelisable: Often — many are greedy or LP-rounding based and parallelise per element/constraint.

def: A strategy for NP-hard optimisation problems that returns a provably near-optimal answer within a guaranteed factor of the true optimum, in polynomial time.
problem: When the exact optimum is intractable, get a solution with a hard quality guarantee rather than an unbounded heuristic.
mechanism: Designs a polynomial-time procedure (greedy, LP relaxation + rounding, local search, primal-dual) plus a proof bounding its output relative to the optimum (e.g. 2-approx vertex cover, (1−1/e) for submodular maximisation).
benefits: Often simple and fast in practice
limits: Some problems are hard to approximate at all (inapproximability)
Constant factors / ratios may be too loose to be useful
failures: Confusing an approximation guarantee with a heuristic with no bound
Using on problems with proven inapproximability
whenNot: A good heuristic with empirical validation suffices and no guarantee is needed
myths: "Approximate = heuristic" — approximation algorithms carry a proven ratio; heuristics do not
